task: I’m getting ready to take my car in for a routine tire check, and I want to make sure I’m not overlooking anything basic before I hand it over. What should I verify on the paperwork and on the car itself so I can catch any mismatch early?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the best way to catch a mismatch early is to compare the **paperwork** to the **actual tire sidewall and vehicle setup** before you leave.

## What to verify on the paperwork
Check:
- tire size
- load index
- speed rating
- brand and model
- quantity installed
- which axle/corner they say each tire went on
- balance / rotation / alignment charges
- TPMS service or relearn note if applicable
- torque / recheck note if listed

## What to verify on the car
Check the tire sidewall for:
- exact size
- load index
- speed rating
- brand/model if you want to confirm substitution
- directional arrow if the tire is directional
- inside/outside marking if it’s asymmetric

Also check:
- all four tires match the intended setup
- the right tire is on the right axle/corner if only some were replaced
- tire pressures match the door placard
- TPMS light is off or explained
- lug nuts look seated normally
- no obvious wheel damage or missing valve caps

## Common mismatch points
- invoice shows one tire spec, sidewall shows another
- tire mounted on the wrong axle
- one tire replaced when more were quoted
- TPMS relearn not done
- wrong rotation pattern for directional or staggered tires

## Best quick check order
1. Sidewall spec
2. Invoice spec
3. Correct axle/corner
4. Pressure / TPMS
5. Torque / recheck note

## Calm follow-up if something looks off
You can say:

> Hi, I compared the paperwork to the tire sidewall and I think there may be a mismatch. Can you confirm the exact size, load index, and speed rating that were installed, and whether they match the vehicle requirements?

If you want, I can give you a **short 5-point checklist** you can read off your phone at the shop.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but I’d want it tighter and a little more specific to avoid a dumb miss.

What I’d check on the paperwork:
- tire size, load index, speed rating
- DOT/date code if they list it
- exact quantity installed
- axle/corner placement
- whether they rotated anything
- torque spec and whether the lugs were rechecked
- TPMS relearn / sensor service if that was needed
- old tire return / disposal line if it matters

On the car itself:
- sidewall size/load/speed matches the invoice
- directional arrow or inside/outside marking is correct
- tread direction looks right if it’s a directional tire
- the valve stems/caps are there
- pressures match the door placard after install
- TPMS light is behaving normally
- lug nuts look even, no obvious cross-threading or uneven seating
- if only one or two tires were changed, the new ones are on the correct axle and matched pair side-to-side

One thing I’d add: check the DOT date code on the actual tire. I’ve had shops get everything else right and still not notice an older tire getting mixed in. Also if it’s a truck or anything with load sensitivity, make sure the load rating isn’t just “close enough.” Close enough is how you end up back there.

If you want, give me the exact tire size and I can turn this into a dead-simple compare list.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, practical check list you can use before you leave.

## On the paperwork, verify:
- tire size
- load index
- speed rating
- DOT/date code if listed
- exact quantity installed
- axle/corner placement
- whether any tires were rotated
- torque spec and whether lugs were rechecked
- TPMS relearn / sensor service if needed
- old tire return / disposal line if it matters

## On the car, verify:
- sidewall size matches the invoice
- load index and speed rating match
- DOT/date code is reasonable and not an odd outlier
- directional arrow or inside/outside marking is correct
- tread direction is correct for directional tires
- valve stems and caps are present
- pressures match the door placard after install
- TPMS light is normal
- lug nuts look evenly seated
- no obvious cross-threading, wheel damage, or stud issues
- if only one or two tires were changed, they’re on the correct axle and matched side-to-side

## Quick order of checks
1. Sidewall size / load / speed  
2. DOT/date code  
3. Correct axle/corner  
4. Directional / inside-outside orientation  
5. Pressure / TPMS  
6. Lug seating / torque note  

## What to ask if something looks off
> Hi, I compared the invoice to the tire sidewall and I think there may be a mismatch. Can you confirm the exact size, load index, speed rating, and DOT/date code that were installed, and whether they match the vehicle requirements?

If you want, I can turn this into a **5-line phone checklist** you can read at the counter.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right direction. I’d still tighten one thing: separate **paperwork errors** from **install errors** so you can catch them fast without standing there reading everything twice.

I’d use it like this:

**Paperwork**
- tire size / load index / speed rating
- DOT/date code if they list it
- exact quantity installed
- axle/corner placement
- rotation done or not
- torque spec + recheck note
- TPMS relearn / sensor service
- disposal/return line if relevant

**Car**
- size/load/speed on sidewall matches invoice
- DOT/date code isn’t the odd one out
- directional arrow or inside/outside marking is correct
- pressure matches door placard after install
- TPMS light behaves normally
- lug nuts seated evenly, no weird gap or cross-thread look
- if only 1–2 tires changed, they’re on the right axle and matched side-to-side

One more thing I’d keep in mind: if they list a torque spec, I’d want to know whether it was final torque or just a reminder. Shops write that stuff loosely sometimes.

I’d still check the date code first. That’s the dumb miss I’d expect most often.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right split. Here’s the cleaner version:

## Paperwork: check for spec / order errors
- tire size
- load index
- speed rating
- DOT/date code if listed
- exact quantity installed
- axle/corner placement
- rotation done or not
- torque spec and whether it was final torque or just a note
- TPMS relearn / sensor service
- disposal / return line if relevant

## Car: check for install errors
- sidewall size/load/speed matches the invoice
- DOT/date code isn’t the odd one out
- directional arrow or inside/outside marking is correct
- pressure matches the door placard after install
- TPMS light behaves normally
- lug nuts seated evenly, no weird gap or cross-thread look
- if only 1–2 tires changed, they’re on the right axle and matched side-to-side

## Best order
1. **Date code**
2. **Sidewall size / load / speed**
3. **Axle / corner placement**
4. **Directional / inside-outside orientation**
5. **Pressure / TPMS**
6. **Lug seating / torque note**

## Why that order
- **Date code** catches the dumb mix-in first
- **Paperwork vs sidewall** catches spec mistakes
- **Axle/corner** catches install mistakes
- **Pressure / TPMS / lug seating** catches the basic finish work

## If something looks off
> Hi, I compared the invoice to the tire sidewall and I think there may be a mismatch. Can you confirm the exact size, load index, speed rating, and DOT/date code that were installed, and whether they match the vehicle requirements?

If you want, I can make this into a **6-line counter checklist** with just the check items and a blank for notes.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically it. I’d still make the counter version a little more brutal and less wordy — something I can scan in 10 seconds.

I’d want it like:

1. **Paperwork spec**: size / load / speed / DOT-date / qty / axle-corner
2. **Install check**: sidewall matches invoice
3. **Orientation**: directional arrow or inside-outside right
4. **Finish work**: pressure / TPMS / lug seating
5. **Torque note**: final torque vs just a reminder
6. **Disposition**: old tire return / disposal if relevant

And I’d keep **date code first** on my own check, like you said. That’s the one that gets missed when everyone’s focused on size and price.

If you make the 6-line counter sheet, I’d want a blank line after each item for the actual numbers. Saves having to flip back and forth.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the **6-line counter sheet** version with blanks:

1. **Paperwork spec:** size / load / speed / DOT-date / qty / axle-corner ________  
2. **Install check:** sidewall matches invoice ________  
3. **Orientation:** directional arrow / inside-outside correct ________  
4. **Finish work:** pressure / TPMS / lug seating ________  
5. **Torque note:** final torque or reminder only ________  
6. **Disposition:** old tire return / disposal if relevant ________  

**Quick personal check order:**  
1. DOT/date code  
2. Sidewall size / load / speed  
3. Axle / corner placement  
4. Orientation  
5. Pressure / TPMS  
6. Lug seating / torque note  

If you want, I can also turn it into a **plain text version you can read off your phone** with no formatting clutter.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s it. Clean enough to use at the counter.

The only thing I’d maybe change is make the blanks wider so I can write the actual numbers without cramming them in. Otherwise it’s solid.

If you do the plain-text phone version, keep the same order. No extra wording.
